Town of Southeast · Putnam County · New York

Southeast Centre

A Neighborhood History — Sodom Road & Brewster Hill Road
"Early colonial settlement on the Philipse Patent. Center of business prior to the coming of the Harlem Railroad and construction of the reservoirs. Early home of the Howes Family, circus performers and impresarios."
— Town of Southeast Historic Sites Commission marker

There is a crossroads in the Town of Southeast, Putnam County, New York, where two old roads meet at the foot of a hill. For a few decades in the 19th century, this intersection was the center of a real community — Southeast Centre, the original town seat, with a Presbyterian church, a parsonage, a Temperance Hall, a carriage and chair factory, a district school, and a cluster of family homes that had been accumulating since before Putnam County even existed.

Then the City of New York arrived. The Croton watershed expansion absorbed vast tracts of land for the reservoir system that still supplies much of New York's water. The neighborhood sits between Bog Brook Reservoir to the north and the East Branch Reservoir — created by the Sodom Dam — to the south and east.

And yet the houses remained. This is their story — told through the deed records of Putnam County, which stretch back without interruption to 1812, and in some cases through earlier Dutchess County records to the Revolutionary era and beyond.

11Properties
240+Years of deeds
1812Putnam Co. formed
1891Reservoir in service

Three properties in this cluster trace their ultimate origin to post-Revolutionary Commissioners of Forfeiture, who sold confiscated Loyalist lands after American independence. All chain of title facts are drawn from physically reviewed deed records — zero citations assumed or inferred.

↑ Tap any lot to explore its history
Filter:
Colonial & early settlers
c.1740David Paddock family arrives from Cape Cod with eight children
Founding
Confirmed from L.H. Roberts' 1876 Brewster Standard centennial address, written with living memory. Children: Nathan, Foster, David, Isaac, Mary, Susannah, Mercy, Sarah. Settled near the Presbyterian meeting house — over a century before William H. Paddock acquires Lot 8 in 1857.
c.1755Capt. Joshua Barnum settles in Southeast
Founding
Confirmed from Roberts 1876 address. His son Stephen was father of "the Hon. D. Barnum, so long County Clerk at Carmel" — the P.D. Barnum who signed as County Clerk through the 1840s in this deed record. Barnum family legal presence spans at least 73 years across three generations.
c.1781–82Commissioners of Forfeiture sell confiscated Loyalist lands
Deed
Post-Revolutionary mechanism that seeds three properties in this cluster. Townsend family receives the Lot 8 predecessor parcel. Paddock family receives Lot 11 predecessor parcels. The Gay Nineties Inn on Route 6 also traces to the same Commissioners. Three Southeast Centre properties all born from the dispossession of Loyalists after the Revolution.
c.1792Moody Howes ("Deacon Moody") builds farmhouse at Sodom Corners
Tradition
Roberts 1876 identifies "Deacon Moody" as "about the first settler at Sodom Corners, now Southeast Centre. He bought all the land in its immediate vicinity." Dwelling confirmed in deed records as early as 1833. Seth B. Howes — Moody's grandson — later rebuilt this into Stonehenge.
Early Putnam County (1812–1858)
June 12, 1812Putnam County created from Dutchess County
Government
Confirmed by the official Putnam County Historian's historical marker (2001) and independently by Roberts 1876. Every property in this cluster holds title through Putnam County records back to this date — and through Dutchess County records before it.
March 12, 1840Q/70 — Daniel Reed 2d reserves the Lot 6 house lot
Deed
Daniel Reed 2d and Abigail sell 100-acre farm to Henry Crosley for $6,300, reserving "a house and lot of land near the foot of the hill." Starting corner: "the south west corner of an old blacksmith shop so called." The 1867 Beers Atlas confirms "B.S.SH" (Blacksmith Shop) at precisely this corner. Solomon Denton, earliest named occupant, was almost certainly the blacksmith. Earliest confirmed Lot 6 reference.
1840–1842Nathan A. Howes — Southeast Town Supervisor and JP
Civic
Also served two terms as State Senator from 1851. Older brother of Seth B. Howes. His prominence in the Lot 11 deed chain reflects civic authority, not merely landholding. Sold the 120-acre Yale Tract to Enos C. Yale in 1858 — the year after his Senate terms ended.
January 29, 1858Nathan A. Howes → Enos C. Yale — 120 acres for $13,000
Deed
Liber 33/192. The land that becomes Lot 11. Yale is a New York City resident at time of purchase. Total Yale watershed awards would eventually reach approximately $23,693 against this $13,000 original purchase price.
Village at its height (1849–1880)
1849Harlem Railroad reaches Southeast — the turning point
Turning Point
Confirmed by Roberts 1876: "about 27 years since." The center of trade shifted to Brewster's Station. Southeast Centre — described as "formerly the centre of town business" — began its long decline. The railroad did not pass through Southeast Centre itself.
June 14, 1853Southeast Centre Presbyterian Church organized
Church
Exact date confirmed from Roberts 1876. "Built in 1853-4, dedicated in June, 1854." Twenty-six members broke away from the Doanesburg congregation. The church lot (Lot 10, 112 Sodom Road) was sold only 37 years later in 1891 for just $104.
1876L.H. Roberts centennial address — Brewster Standard
Primary Source
Written with living memory, nine years after the 1867 Beers map. Confirms: "Deacon Moody" as first settler at Sodom Corners; David Paddock from Cape Cod ~1740; Capt. Joshua Barnum from 1755; Wm. C. Waring as "a native of this town, now a resident of Yonkers"; church organized June 14, 1853; reservoir construction already underway in western part of town. Southeast Centre called "Hatesville" in earlier historical records.
Watershed era (1880–1901)
July 22, 1887Brewster Standard — formal City of New York aqueduct appraisal notice
Primary Source
Appraisal maps already filed May 17, 1887 at Putnam County Clerk's office. Named landowners: Lydia A. Yale (3 appearances), Warren S. Paddock, Stephen C. Barnum (2), Violetta Birch (= Violette Birch, 1890 Howes estate executor), George Cole (= G. Cole on 1867 Beers map). Same page: Abagail Warren advertising her property for sale; James D. Baxter signing as Town Assessor; Frederic S. Barnum announcing his dual NYC/Brewster practice.
1888–1891East Branch Reservoir construction — put in service 1891
Watershed
Construction began 1888. Town of Southeast Historic Sites Commission marker confirms: "Put in service in 1891 & connected by tunnel to Bog Brook Reservoir to impound nearly 10 million gallons of clean water." Full civil works completed 1893 per Aqueduct Commissioners Report. Dam sat directly south and east of Stonehenge.
1889Johnstown Flood; death at the dam; carriage factory demolished
Watershed
May 1889: Johnstown Flood (2,000+ lives) made dam-burst fears real. Fall 1889: Rocco Giuseppe Pellettiere, 34-year-old Italian immigrant, killed at East Branch dam construction when a steel cable snapped. December 6, 1889: Brewster Standard reports carriage factory (Wright & Co.) demolished — 21 families displaced, 1,471 acres taken.
September 12, 1892Baxter → Howes: the Morningthorpe land purchase ($12,500)
Deed
Liber 74/120–123. James D. & Emma O. Baxter sell ~99 acres on Turk Hill to Seth B. Howes for $12,500. Three parcels bounded by Turk Hill highways — entirely separate from Sodom Road. Tenant George Sloat farming under lease to April 1894. Land purchase only — Howes built Morningthorpe Castle on this ground. Notarized by Frederic S. Barnum.
September 10, 1901Same-day dual recording — church lot to school district
Deed
Frederic S. Barnum's most architecturally elegant legal construction. Emma O. Baxter → School District No. 6 ($300) recorded at 10 a.m. John R. Yale → Emma O. Baxter recorded at noon — same day, same page. School served until 1927. Clerestory windows in the current 112 Sodom Road structure match the c.1900 school photograph.

Hello! I'm your guide to Southeast Centre — the neighborhood at Sodom Road and Brewster Hill Road in the Town of Southeast, Putnam County. I know the deed research for all eleven properties in detail: ownership chains going back to the 1700s in some cases, the families who lived here, and the story of how the City of New York's reservoir system transformed this village. Southeast Centre: A Neighborhood History  ·  Town of Southeast, Putnam County, New York
Research by deed chain analysis  ·  March 2026  ·  Version 18
All chain of title facts drawn from physically reviewed primary source deed records
Southeast Centre · Town of Southeast · Putnam County, New York

A Village That Almost Disappeared

For a few decades in the 19th century, Southeast Centre was the beating heart of the Town of Southeast — a genuine village with churches, schools, factories, and families going back before the county itself existed. Then the City of New York arrived, and everything changed.

The Founding

Looking back 200 years to discover what Southeast Centre was like takes a fair amount of research — and perhaps a bit of imagination. We learn that settlers were already here in small numbers in the 1730s. The territory was known as The Oblong, an approximate two-mile strip extending from Long Island Sound to the Massachusetts border, where settlers could buy land and receive an indisputable title.

Among the earliest settlers was Moody Howes — "Treadwell Moody, as he was familiarly called," wrote H.H. Roberts in his 1876 centennial address to the town, "was about the first settler at Sodom Corners, now Southeast Centre. He bought all the land in its immediate vicinity." He came from Cape Cod and built his farmhouse at the foot of Brewster Hill Road around 1792. His descendant Seth B. Howes would later rebuild that same farmhouse into the eclectic Victorian mansion still standing today as Stonehenge.

David Paddock — grandfather to later generations of the family that would anchor this neighborhood — came from Cape Cod around 1744 with eight children and settled near the Presbyterian meeting house. The Crane family built a mill two miles north. Capt. Joshua Barnum came to the town around 1755 and settled on the west side of the East Branch of the Croton River. Their children and grandchildren would run the legal and commercial life of Southeast Centre for the better part of a century.

Putnam County was created from Dutchess County on June 12, 1812. The first deed recorded in the new county was September 9th of that year. Many of the families who appear in this neighborhood's history were already established landholders when the county was formed.

The Village at Its Height

It is difficult to determine exactly when the streets of Southeast Centre were named. The Beers map of 1867 shows the hub of the village with four streets meeting at "Sodom Corners." One ran south toward what is now Brewster Village. The road to the west was variously named — once Howes Street, again Tattle Street, and finally Brewster Hill Road. To the east, Church Street (now Sodom Road) climbed the hill where the Presbyterian Church and the Temperance Hall were located. A fourth street, Foster Avenue, is now a lane leading directly to the water's edge of Bog Brook Reservoir.

By the 1850s and 1860s, Southeast Centre was a small but genuine commercial and manufacturing center. The 1867 Beers map shows, alongside the familiar residential labels, a Carriage and Chair Factory (Wright & Co.) at the intersection, a Temperance Hall on Church Street, and a J.A. Howes Hat Factory at Sodom Corners. The map's business advertising directory confirms further: J.H. Smith was a Physician and Surgeon; Burch and Beers were Manufacturers of Fur Hats; Geo. Cole was a Carriage and Wagon Maker; S. Reed manufactured Boots and Shoes; and Rogers and Hoyt were Tanners and Dealers in Leather.

The wool manufacturing business was organized around 1859 by William C. Waring — a native of this town, later a resident of Yonkers — who was succeeded by O.W. Budd, who for years conducted the business and served as Supervisor of the town. The Southeast Centre Presbyterian Church was organized June 14, 1853, its building begun that same year and dedicated June 28, 1854. The Washington Ball of February 20, 1857 was held at C.B. Howe's Hall at Southeast Centre — with George Hine on the invitation committee — drawing guests from Southeast, Patterson, North Salem, Somers, Yorktown, and Mill Plain. This was the neighborhood at its social height.

The Railroad and the Shift

The first great change came not from New York City but from a railroad. The Harlem Railroad reached the area in 1849, and the town changed. As the Brewster family developed the new village at Brewster's Station to the east, Southeast Centre slowly decreased in importance. Roads that had radiated from Sodom Corners — toward Westchester, toward Crane's mills, toward Milltown — began to feed a new center of gravity.

In 1863, the Hedingville Methodist Episcopal Church removed to a new location on the Main Street of the new village. In 1880, the Presbyterian congregation followed, resolving to build a new church in Brewster village. They dedicated it on June 15, 1886, the same year they obtained a court order to sell their old parsonage on Sodom Road and petitioned for authorization to sell the church lot itself. Both institutional properties were shed simultaneously. The congregation that had anchored Southeast Centre for thirty years was gone.

The financial cost of that departure tells its own story. The congregation sold the old parsonage for $2,250, used the proceeds to buy a Brewster village lot for $3,680, mortgaged that too, and ultimately sold it for only $750 — a net loss of roughly $2,930 on the Brewster venture. The church lot at 112 Sodom Road was sold in 1891 for just $104. The building that replaced it — the new district schoolhouse of 1901 — still stands, its distinctive row of high narrow clerestory windows matching the "Opening of a new school at Southeast Center, c.1900" photograph in the town history.

Even the arrival of the Borden Condensed Milk Factory near Hedingville in 1863 — of great importance to the material interests of the town, condensing from 20,000 to 30,000 quarts of milk per day — did not bring new settlers into Southeast Centre itself, only into the developing village area around it.

The Watershed — How New York Changed Everything

The second great change was not gradual. It was engineered by the City of New York. Beginning in the 1870s and accelerating through the 1890s, the city's Croton watershed expansion absorbed vast tracts of Putnam County land for the reservoir system that still supplies much of New York's water. Five reservoirs lie within the Town of Southeast's borders today. Southeast Centre sits between two of them: Bog Brook Reservoir to the north and northwest, and the East Branch Reservoir — created by the Sodom Dam — to the south and east.

The dam was under construction from 1888. It sat directly below Stonehenge, the Howes family mansion at 10 Brewster Hill Road. The Johnstown Flood of May 1889 — which claimed more than 2,000 lives when the South Fork Dam collapsed in Pennsylvania — made the fear of a dam burst viscerally real. A construction death at the East Branch dam that same fall brought the danger home: Rocco Giuseppe Pellettiere, a 34-year-old Italian immigrant, was killed when a steel cable snapped.

Seth B. Howes and his wife Amy moved to higher ground at Morningthorpe Castle on Turk Hill around 1892–93. On December 6, 1889, the Brewster Standard reported that "a carriage factory must be torn down and removed" as part of the reservoir construction — 21 families displaced, 1,471 acres taken. The Wright & Co. Carriage and Chair Factory at the center of the village, shown so prominently on the 1867 Beers map, was demolished as the reservoir advanced.

The flooding of pasture lands changed the face of the landscape and, over the years, brought about the decline of the dairy industry. The population of Putnam County declined so quickly that by 1920 there were fewer inhabitants than in 1820. Real estate values stagnated: 104 Sodom Road sold for $875 in 1857, $900 in 1908 after 51 years, and $820 at a foreclosure sale in 1914.

What Remained

What the watershed era could not do was erase the physical record. The houses remained. The stone walls remained. The deed chains remained — stretching back without interruption to 1812, and in many cases through Dutchess County records to the Revolutionary era and beyond.

The Reed family had owned the homestead at 96–98 Sodom Road since at least 1840 — their tenure anchoring the neighborhood through its entire commercial life and into the watershed era. Solomon Denton had been there before them. The Paddock family had held 104 Sodom Road since 1857. The Scribner family had owned 108 Sodom Road in their own name since 1866. The Yale family had owned the 120-acre tract at 116 Sodom Road since 1858, their name appearing in the deed descriptions of every surrounding property for decades.

One detail from the research crystallizes the human cost of the watershed era. James D. Wright — almost certainly the principal of Wright & Co., the Carriage and Chair Factory at the intersection — served as witness and notary on the April 1893 Yale watershed deed, formalizing the City of New York's acquisition of the land adjacent to his factory. He signed the instrument that absorbed his own business.

A second wave of physical disruption followed in the early 1970s, when the widening of Route 22 and its transition into I-684 further altered the former Church Street landscape. The neighborhood that time-traveling visitors from the 1860s would have recognized had, by that point, been reshaped twice over.

And yet a visitor today can still stand at the intersection of Sodom Road and Brewster Hill Road, read the historic marker erected by the Town of Southeast Historic Sites Commission — "Early colonial settlement on the Philipse Patent. Center of business prior to the coming of the Harlem Railroad and construction of the reservoirs. Early home of the Howes Family, circus performers and impresarios" — and look up Brewster Hill Road toward the stone wall of Stonehenge. The same wall that Seth B. Howes built when he came home from Chicago to rebuild his grandfather's farmhouse into a Victorian mansion, before the reservoir drove him uphill to higher ground. Southeast Centre (Sodom), however, remains a beautiful and historic section of the Town of Southeast.

Research by deed chain analysis — March 2026. All chain of title facts drawn from physically reviewed primary source deed records. Narrative informed by the 1867 Beers map, the 1876 centennial address of H.H. Roberts to the Town of Southeast, the published history of the Town of Southeast, and contemporaneous Brewster Standard newspaper sources.
